_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Chanson & Salenave 2008 PMID 18760715 and Dekkers et al. 2008. Treatment evidence from Endocrine Society guidelines 2014 (PMID 24915290). IGF-1 normalization surgery rates from Nomikos et al. JCEM 2005 (PMID 15840736). Somatostatin analogue data from Melmed et al. 2009.

h1Title: Acromegaly: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acromegaly is a rare endocrine disorder caused by sustained excess secretion of growth hormone (GH), almost always from a benign somatotroph adenoma of the anterior pituitary gland, that drives supraphysiological production of insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) and stimulates pathological growth of bone, soft tissue, and organs. The global prevalence is approximately 40-70 cases per million; diagnosis is delayed an average of 7-10 years after symptom onset because changes are gradual. The cardinal features — progressive enlargement of the hands, feet, jaw, and forehead — are recognizable in retrospect but subtle in the early years. Untreated acromegaly shortens life expectancy by 10-15 years, primarily from cardiovascular, respiratory, and metabolic complications. Transsphenoidal pituitary surgery is first-line treatment, achieving biochemical remission in 50-90% of microadenomas and normalizing IGF-1 in 40-60% of macroadenomas.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 40-70 cases per million population (global prevalence); annual incidence 3-4 per million (Endocrine Society 2014)
keyStats.demographics: Affects men and women approximately equally; peak onset age 40-50 years in adults; pituitary gigantism (excess GH before growth plate fusion) occurs predominantly in males under 20
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis 40-50 years; average diagnostic delay 7-10 years from first symptom to diagnosis
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 20,000-30,000 people living with acromegaly in the United States; global total estimated 250,000-350,000

definition:
Acromegaly and pituitary gigantism (ICD-10: E22.0) are both caused by excess growth hormone secretion from the pituitary gland, differing only in whether the excess begins before (gigantism) or after (acromegaly) growth plate fusion in adulthood.

In acromegaly, which accounts for more than 95% of cases, a benign monoclonal somatotroph adenoma secretes GH autonomously. GH stimulates hepatic synthesis of IGF-1, which is the key mediator of tissue overgrowth. Elevated IGF-1 drives periosteal bone expansion, soft tissue hypertrophy, organomegaly (cardiomegaly, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly), and metabolic dysregulation including insulin resistance. Approximately 5% of somatotroph adenomas arise in the context of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), Carney complex, or McCune-Albright syndrome. Ectopic GH secretion from carcinoid tumors or GH-releasing hormone (GHRH) excess from hypothalamic tumors or lung tumors causes fewer than 2% of cases.

Diagnosis requires demonstration of elevated IGF-1 for age and sex combined with failure to suppress GH below 1 ng/mL (or below 0.4 ng/mL with modern ultrasensitive assays) during an oral glucose tolerance test. MRI of the pituitary gland identifies the adenoma, which is a macroadenoma (over 10 mm) in approximately 70-80% of patients at diagnosis. Management is led by endocrinologists, neurosurgeons with pituitary expertise, and radiation oncology for residual or recurrent disease.

typesClassification.description: GH excess occurring after growth plate fusion in adults. Produces progressive acral and facial changes without increase in linear height; most patients are diagnosed 5-15 years after onset. This is the dominant form, accounting for over 95% of E22.0 diagnoses.

GH excess beginning before growth plate fusion, causing markedly excessive linear height gain. Giant pituitary adenomas (often AIP-mutation-associated), diffuse somatotroph hyperplasia, or Carney complex are common underlying causes. Linear height may reach 7-8 feet. Treated with the same modalities as acromegaly but earlier intervention is critical to limit height overgrowth.

Approximately 5-10% of acromegaly cases occur as part of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), a germline MEN1 gene mutation syndrome. These patients also have primary hyperparathyroidism and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Genetic testing is warranted in patients under 30 or with family history of pituitary tumors.

Less than 2% of cases are caused by ectopic GHRH secretion from pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, bronchial carcinoids, or hypothalamic gangliocytomas, stimulating normal somatotrophs to hyperplasia and excess GH. Identified by elevated plasma GHRH levels and absence of pituitary adenoma on MRI.

primarySymptoms: Gradual, progressive enlargement of the hands and feet — patients notice ring tightening, inability to remove rings, and increasing shoe size by 1-2 sizes over several years, often the first reported symptom.

Coarsening of facial features with frontal bossing (prominent supraorbital ridges), prognathism (lower jaw protrusion), widening of the nose, and macroglossia (enlarged tongue), most recognizable when comparing photographs over 5-10 years.
Hyperhidrosis (excessive sweating) with offensive body odor in over 60% of patients, resulting from sweat gland hypertrophy driven by GH and IGF-1.

Carpal tunnel syndrome with bilateral hand paresthesia, numbness, and weakness — the most common peripheral nerve complication, found in approximately 20-35% of patients, caused by soft tissue compression of the median nerve at the wrist.
Arthralgia and joint stiffness, particularly in the hips, knees, and spine, from periosteal bone overgrowth and cartilage hypertrophy; accelerated osteoarthritis is a long-term complication.
Headaches in approximately 60% of patients, often described as frontotemporal, caused by direct pituitary tumor expansion or increased intracranial pressure.
Visual field defects — classically bitemporal hemianopia — when a pituitary macroadenoma compresses the optic chiasm, found in approximately 20-30% of macroadenoma patients at diagnosis.
Fatigue and decreased stamina from cardiomyopathy, sleep apnea, and metabolic disruption; obstructive sleep apnea occurs in 60-80% of acromegaly patients.
Sexual dysfunction — decreased libido, oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea in women, erectile dysfunction in men — from concomitant hyperprolactinemia or hypopituitarism in approximately 30-40% of cases.
Galactorrhea and menstrual irregularity in women when the somatotroph adenoma co-secretes prolactin, which occurs in approximately 25% of cases.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive ring or glove tightening despite no weight gain, or a documented shoe size increase by 1 size or more in an adult — classic early harbingers that predate visible facial changes by years.
Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome without obvious explanation in a middle-aged adult, particularly with concurrent jaw changes or facial coarsening.
New or worsening snoring and sleep apnea in an otherwise healthy adult, suggesting macroglossia and pharyngeal soft tissue enlargement driven by IGF-1.

Unexplained blood glucose elevation or new type 2 diabetes mellitus in a patient whose habitus does not explain insulin resistance — GH is a counter-regulatory hormone that directly causes insulin resistance.
emergencySigns: Acute visual loss or new bitemporal visual field defect with severe headache — pituitary apoplexy (hemorrhage into or infarction of the pituitary adenoma) is a medical emergency requiring neurosurgical consultation within hours.
Sudden severe headache, nausea, vomiting, and meningismus in a known acromegaly patient — pituitary apoplexy presentation mandating urgent MRI and possible emergency decompression surgery.
New-onset hypertensive crisis or acute heart failure with suspected acromegalic cardiomyopathy — requires urgent cardiology evaluation alongside endocrine management.
Rapidly deteriorating vision or loss of direct pupillary light reflex in a patient with known pituitary macroadenoma — requires emergency ophthalmology and neurosurgery evaluation.

causes.cause: Benign pituitary somatotroph adenoma (95% of cases)
causes.description: A monoclonal expansion of pituitary somatotroph cells produces a GH-secreting adenoma. Approximately 70-80% are macroadenomas (diameter over 10 mm) at diagnosis due to the long pre-diagnosis interval. Mutations in the GNAS gene (Gsα protein) constitutively activate adenylate cyclase and cAMP signaling, driving autonomous GH secretion in approximately 40% of sporadic cases.

causes.cause: Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1 gene mutation)
causes.description: Germline mutations in the MEN1 tumor suppressor gene predispose to pituitary adenomas (including somatotroph), parathyroid adenomas, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. MEN1-associated acromegaly tends to present at younger ages (under 35) and may be multifocal within the pituitary.

causes.cause: AIP (aryl hydrocarbon receptor-interacting protein) gene mutation
causes.description: Germline AIP mutations are found in approximately 15-20% of familial isolated pituitary adenoma (FIPA) cases and in 10-15% of young-onset sporadic acromegaly patients under 30. AIP-mutated tumors are typically larger, more aggressive, and less responsive to somatostatin analogues than sporadic tumors.

Rare cause — less than 1-2% of cases — where a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor, bronchial carcinoid, or hypothalamic gangliocytoma secretes GHRH, stimulating pituitary somatotroph hyperplasia. GHRH levels above 300 pg/mL on plasma assay and a symmetrically enlarged but not focally adenomatous pituitary on MRI raise this diagnosis.

An autosomal dominant disorder causing primary pigmented nodular adrenocortical disease, cardiac myxomas, spotty skin pigmentation, and pituitary somatotroph or somatomammotroph adenomas. Acromegaly in Carney complex accounts for approximately 10% of affected patients and is managed the same way as sporadic disease.

riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of MEN1 patients have a 50% probability of carrying the germline MEN1 mutation; all with confirmed mutation should undergo pituitary MRI every 3 years from diagnosis.
AIP mutation carriers have a 20-30% lifetime risk of developing a pituitary adenoma, most commonly somatotroph; testing recommended in young-onset (under 30) acromegaly or with family history.

riskFactors.factor: Young male sex for pituitary gigantism
riskFactors.description: Pre-pubertal GH excess (gigantism) is more common in males; AIP and X-linked acrogigantism (GPR101 gene duplication) are particular causes in young males under 10.
Patients with prior pituitary disease, including prior prolactinoma or non-functioning adenoma, have an elevated background risk of GH-secreting somatotroph co-adenoma or recurrence.
Approximately 40% of sporadic somatotroph adenomas harbor a somatic GNAS mutation (Gsα) in the tumor only, not germline; this is an intrinsic tumor driver rather than an inherited predisposition.

affectedDemographics: Men and women are equally affected by acromegaly; median age at diagnosis is 40-50 years, though the onset is typically 7-10 years earlier.
Pituitary gigantism predominantly affects boys and young males; GPR101 gene duplication on the X chromosome specifically causes X-linked acrogigantism in infants and young children.
Patients with MEN1 syndrome develop pituitary adenomas (10-40% of MEN1 carriers) at a median age of 30, about a decade younger than sporadic acromegaly.
European and North American registries show no strong ethnic skew; under-diagnosis in lower-income countries likely results in underestimated global prevalence.
diagnosisOverview:
The diagnostic algorithm for acromegaly begins with clinical suspicion based on the characteristic phenotype — enlarged hands, feet, and face — and proceeds to biochemical confirmation before structural imaging.

First-line biochemical test: IGF-1 level, measured fasting and in a stable medical state, compared against age- and sex-matched normal ranges. A single elevated IGF-1 is present in over 97% of active acromegaly cases. However, IGF-1 is also elevated in puberty and pregnancy and is reduced in liver disease and malnutrition, so clinical context matters.

Confirmatory test: oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with 75 g glucose; GH is measured at 0, 30, 60, 90, and 120 minutes. In normal subjects, GH suppresses to below 1 ng/mL (or below 0.4 ng/mL by ultrasensitive assay). Failure to suppress confirms autonomous GH secretion. Random GH measurements are unreliable due to pulsatile GH secretion.

Structural imaging: MRI of the pituitary with and without gadolinium contrast is performed after biochemical confirmation. The adenoma is a macroadenoma (over 10 mm) in 70-80% of cases; microadenomas (under 10 mm) are found in 20-30%. Cavernous sinus invasion or optic chiasm compression identified on MRI influences surgical planning and prognosis.

Additional investigations at diagnosis include: visual field testing (Humphrey perimetry) when MRI shows suprasellar extension; assessment of other pituitary hormone deficiencies (LH, FSH, TSH, ACTH axes); fasting glucose and HbA1c; echocardiography; sleep study; colonoscopy (polyp risk elevated); bone density measurement; prolactin level (co-secreted in 25% of tumors).

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line screening test; elevated IGF-1 for age and sex is present in over 97% of active acromegaly
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single fasting blood draw from an arm vein. Laboratories compare the result against age- and sex-specific reference ranges. Results in 1-3 days. A normal IGF-1 effectively excludes active acromegaly.

diagnosticTests.test: Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with GH measurement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms failure of GH suppression — the defining biochemical criterion for acromegaly; distinguishes from physiological GH elevation in obesity and puberty
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After an overnight fast, a baseline blood sample is taken. You drink a 75 g glucose solution. Blood is drawn at 30, 60, 90, and 120 minutes. In acromegaly, GH does not fall below 1 ng/mL. Takes about 2 hours; bring reading material.

diagnosticTests.test: Pituitary MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the adenoma, determines size (micro vs macroadenoma), assesses invasion of cavernous sinus and proximity to optic chiasm
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in an MRI scanner. Contrast dye injected into a vein during the scan. No radiation. Patients with claustrophobia may need mild sedation. Results reviewed by an experienced neuroradiologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies bitemporal hemianopia or other visual field defects from optic chiasm compression by a suprasellar macroadenoma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Each eye is tested separately: you press a button each time you see a flash of light in your peripheral vision. Takes 15-20 minutes per eye. Used at baseline and after surgery to confirm visual recovery.

diagnosticTests.test: Pituitary hormone profile (prolactin, LH, FSH, TSH, ACTH, cortisol)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypopituitarism or co-secretion of prolactin, which alters medical management
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Multiple blood tests from one draw, timed at 9 a.m. for cortisol accuracy. Results in 1-5 days. Defines whether hormone replacement is needed and whether a somatotroph adenoma is mixed (GH plus prolactin).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for acromegalic cardiomyopathy — concentric left ventricular hypertrophy and biventricular diastolic dysfunction found in 60-80% of active acromegaly
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless ultrasound scan of the heart. Probe is applied to the chest with cool gel. Takes 30-40 minutes. Detects hypertrophy and valve disease that require management independently of the pituitary tumor.

treatmentOverview:
The 2014 Endocrine Society clinical practice guidelines (Katznelson et al.) define treatment success as normalization of IGF-1 for age and sex, random GH below 1 ng/mL (or OGTT-suppressed GH below 0.4 ng/mL by ultrasensitive assay), and relief of tumor mass effects. Transsphenoidal surgery (TSS) is the recommended first-line treatment for the majority of patients, offering immediate reduction in GH levels and rapid tumor decompression.

Transsphenoidal surgery: neurosurgeons access the pituitary gland through the nasal passages and sphenoid sinus under general anesthesia. For microadenomas, biochemical remission is achieved in 75-90% of cases when performed by high-volume pituitary surgeons; for macroadenomas without cavernous sinus invasion, remission rates are 40-65%. Cavernous sinus invasion limits complete surgical resection in approximately 30-40% of macroadenomas.

Somatostatin receptor ligands (SRL): octreotide LAR (long-acting release) or lanreotide autogel are subcutaneous or intramuscular injections given every 4 weeks, providing IGF-1 normalization in 40-65% of patients and GH control in 50-70%. SRLs are used as primary therapy in patients unfit for surgery, as adjuvant therapy after incomplete surgery, and to shrink adenomas before surgery (pre-operative shrinkage of 30-50% in 20-30% of cases).

Pegvisomant: a GH receptor antagonist (subcutaneous daily injection) that blocks GH action directly, normalizing IGF-1 in up to 95% of patients regardless of GH level. It does not reduce the tumor and requires regular liver enzyme monitoring. Used for SRL-resistant cases.

GH dopamine agonists (cabergoline): active in the approximately 30-35% of patients whose adenomas co-secrete prolactin; IGF-1 normalization in 20-35% of unselected patients with weekly oral cabergoline.

Radiosurgery and stereotactic radiotherapy: reserved for patients with persistent disease after surgery and medical therapy. Gamma Knife and CyberKnife stereotactic radiosurgery achieve biochemical remission in 40-60% of patients at 5-10 years but carry a risk of hypopituitarism in approximately 30-50% over 10 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Octreotide LAR (20-40 mg IM every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Long-acting somatostatin analogue that suppresses GH secretion by binding somatostatin receptor subtype 2 on somatotroph cells. Requires dose titration based on IGF-1 response at 3 monthly intervals. Common side effects: gallstone formation (10-20%), gastrointestinal bloating, and blood glucose dysregulation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IGF-1 normalization in 40-65% of patients; GH below 2.5 ng/mL in 50-70%; tumor volume reduction of more than 20% in 20-30% of patients (Melmed et al. JCEM 2005)

medicalTreatments.name: Lanreotide autogel (60-120 mg SC every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Formulated for patient self-injection via deep subcutaneous route; equivalent efficacy to octreotide LAR. Preferred in patients who wish to self-administer. Same monitoring schedule and side-effect profile as octreotide LAR.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to octreotide LAR in direct comparison studies; similar 40-60% IGF-1 normalization rate

medicalTreatments.name: Pegvisomant (10-30 mg SC daily)
medicalTreatments.description: GH receptor antagonist that competitively blocks GH action at peripheral tissues, reducing IGF-1 synthesis without reducing GH levels. IGF-1 monitoring is used to titrate dose. Does not act on the tumor. Liver enzyme elevation in approximately 2-3% requires monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IGF-1 normalization in 90-97% of patients in clinical trials; largest head-to-head advantage over SRLs in patients with insulin resistance and diabetes

medicalTreatments.name: Cabergoline (0.5-3.5 mg orally twice weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Dopamine D2 receptor agonist; effective in GH-PRL co-secreting adenomas. Lower cost than SRLs; oral administration. Less effective as monotherapy for pure GH adenomas. Combined with SRL for synergistic IGF-1 lowering in partial SRL responders.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IGF-1 normalization in 20-35% of unselected patients; higher in mixed GH-PRL adenomas (up to 50%)

medicalTreatments.name: Transsphenoidal surgery (surgical intervention, primary therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: Endoscopic endonasal or microscopic transsphenoidal approach via the sphenoid sinus to the sella turcica. Tumor debulking relieves mass effects immediately and reduces GH-IGF-1 levels; cure is achieved when complete adenoma excision is possible.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microadenoma remission 75-90%; macroadenoma remission 40-65% without cavernous sinus invasion; 10-40% with invasion (Nomikos et al. JCEM 2005)

medicalTreatments.name: Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife)
medicalTreatments.description: Single high-dose focused radiation delivered to pituitary tumor remnant after surgery and medical therapy. Effective for well-defined tumors at least 3-5 mm from the optic apparatus. Biochemical remission occurs gradually over 3-10 years.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Biochemical remission 40-60% at 5-10 years; hypopituitarism risk 30-50% at 10 years post-treatment

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic transsphenoidal adenomectomy
surgicalOptions.description: First-line surgical treatment: neurosurgeon accesses the sella through the nostril and sphenoid sinus using an endoscope. No facial incision. In specialized pituitary centers, perioperative mortality is below 0.5%; CSF leak complication rate 2-5%; permanent diabetes insipidus 1-5%.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Microadenoma (under 10 mm): remission 75-90% in high-volume centers; macroadenoma without cavernous sinus invasion: 40-65%; with invasion: 10-40%

surgicalOptions.name: Microscopic transsphenoidal adenomectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Traditional approach using a microscope rather than endoscope; equivalent outcomes to endoscopic approach in experienced hands. Choice between approaches depends on surgeon expertise and tumor anatomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Equivalent to endoscopic approach in comparative series; individual center outcomes vary most by surgeon volume rather than technique

surgicalOptions.name: Craniotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely required for large invasive adenomas with significant suprasellar extension not accessible transsphenoidally, or for recurrent tumors with unusual anatomy. Higher morbidity than transsphenoidal approach; reserved for select cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Used as adjunct or rescue procedure; biochemical cure rates lower than primary transsphenoidal surgery due to larger residual disease at time of surgery

whySeeSpecialist: Acromegaly must be managed by a multidisciplinary team including an endocrinologist, a neurosurgeon with dedicated pituitary expertise, and often an ophthalmologist, cardiologist, and sleep medicine physician. The complexity of biochemical monitoring, surgical decision-making, and long-term surveillance for cancer and cardiovascular disease makes this essential. Referral to a pituitary center that performs more than 50 transsphenoidal operations per year is associated with significantly better surgical cure rates.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an endocrinologist who manages acromegaly regularly and has access to a pituitary multidisciplinary team. For surgery, choose a neurosurgeon who performs at least 50 pituitary operations annually — high surgical volume is the strongest predictor of remission. Pituitary centers affiliated with academic medical institutions typically have access to Gamma Knife radiosurgery, pegvisomant protocols, and the full range of diagnostic endocrinology including dynamic testing.
hospitalCriteria: Pituitary center with a dedicated multidisciplinary team including endocrinology, neurosurgery, neuroradiology, ophthalmology, and radiation oncology
Availability of dynamic pituitary hormone testing (OGTT, insulin tolerance test) and high-sensitivity GH assays
Neurosurgeon performing at least 50 pituitary operations per year for optimal remission rates
Stereotactic radiosurgery unit for residual or recurrent disease management
24-hour endocrine emergency cover for pituitary apoplexy management
keyFacilities: Tertiary pituitary centers at academic medical institutions
Endocrine outpatient clinics with acromegaly monitoring protocols
Gamma Knife or CyberKnife stereotactic radiosurgery units
Cardiac imaging and sleep medicine services for complication monitoring
preventionStrategies: Genetic counseling and surveillance for MEN1 syndrome, AIP mutation carriers, and Carney complex probands; pituitary MRI every 3 years in confirmed mutation carriers reduces diagnostic delay.
Awareness of familial pituitary adenoma kindreds: family members of patients with AIP mutations should undergo testing and enter surveillance programs.

Early medical evaluation when adults notice progressive ring or shoe-size changes, bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, or sleep apnea — reducing the average 7-10 year diagnostic delay is the most impactful preventive measure.
lifestyleModifications: Attend all scheduled endocrinology reviews and GH-IGF-1 monitoring visits, typically every 3-6 months during active treatment and annually after remission.

Manage cardiovascular risk factors aggressively: acromegaly independently doubles cardiovascular mortality; control hypertension, dyslipidemia, and diabetes mellitus with targets appropriate for high-risk patients.
Undergo annual colonoscopy or 5-yearly if polyp-free after age 40 — patients with acromegaly have a 2-fold increased risk of colorectal cancer and accelerated polyp growth.
Treat sleep apnea with CPAP therapy; uncontrolled sleep apnea worsens cardiovascular and metabolic risk substantially in acromegaly.
Physical therapy and joint-protective exercises for acromegalic arthropathy; low-impact activities — swimming, cycling — preserve joint function while limiting cartilage stress.
Maintain a healthy weight; obesity exacerbates IGF-1 levels, insulin resistance, and sleep apnea — all of which are independently elevated in acromegaly.

recommended: Low glycemic index diet to manage glucose intolerance and insulin resistance, present in 30-60% of patients with active disease
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation (1,000-1,200 mg calcium, 800-2,000 IU vitamin D daily) given accelerated bone turnover and fracture risk in treated acromegaly
High-fiber diet to reduce colorectal cancer risk — polyp formation is accelerated in acromegaly patients
Mediterranean-pattern diet to address the doubled cardiovascular risk from cardiomyopathy, hypertension, and dyslipidemia
avoid: High-sugar foods and refined carbohydrates that worsen GH-induced insulin resistance and hyperglycemia
Excessive alcohol which compounds liver stress from pegvisomant treatment and contributes to hypertension

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact aerobic exercise — swimming, cycling, walking — is recommended for cardiovascular conditioning and weight management. High-impact activities and heavy resistance training should be approached cautiously given accelerated osteoarthritis and risk of vertebral fracture in treated disease. Consult a physiotherapist familiar with acromegalic joint disease before starting a new exercise program. After surgical remission, exercise tolerance typically improves significantly as cardiac function normalizes over 1-2 years.
dailyManagement: Inject somatostatin analogue or pegvisomant on schedule and record the site and date to rotate injection sites and prevent lipohypertrophy.
Monitor blood glucose fasting twice per week during active disease; somatostatin analogues increase blood glucose in some patients by inhibiting insulin secretion.

Track headaches, visual changes, and any recurrence of hand or foot swelling — soft tissue regression after successful treatment is slow (months to years) but progressive enlargement on treatment warrants reassessment.
Report new or worsening joint pain to the endocrinologist; acromegalic arthropathy may require rheumatology co-management or joint replacement referral.
Measure blood pressure at every clinic visit; hypertension persists in approximately 40-50% of patients even after biochemical remission and requires independent management.
prognosis: With biochemical remission, life expectancy in acromegaly returns close to that of the general population. Patients achieving GH below 1 ng/mL and normal IGF-1 with treatment have standardized mortality ratios of approximately 1.0-1.3 compared with 2.0-4.0 in uncontrolled disease. The principal causes of excess mortality — cardiovascular disease, respiratory disease, and malignancy — decline substantially but do not fully normalize, particularly if comorbidities such as diabetes or hypertension were already established before remission. Surgical remission rates of 75-90% for microadenomas and 40-65% for non-invasive macroadenomas mean that most patients achieve control with surgery alone. Approximately 30-40% require adjuvant medical therapy with SRLs or pegvisomant for ongoing control. Colorectal cancer risk remains elevated even after remission and justifies ongoing surveillance colonoscopy. Arthropathy and sleep apnea often improve but rarely fully resolve, requiring long-term specialist input.
recoveryTimeline: GH levels fall within hours of successful surgery; IGF-1 normalizes within weeks to months of achieving GH control. Soft tissue changes regress slowly over months to years: hand and foot size decrease modestly; facial features coarsen less but do not fully reverse. Carpal tunnel syndrome often resolves within 3-12 months of GH normalization. Cardiomyopathy shows echocardiographic improvement within 6-12 months of biochemical remission. Sleep apnea may persist despite remission and require continued CPAP therapy.
complications: Acromegalic cardiomyopathy — concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, and arrhythmias present in 60-80% at diagnosis; the leading cause of early mortality; detected by echocardiography and managed with ACE inhibitors or beta-blockers.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus and insulin resistance — found in 30-60% of patients at diagnosis due to direct counter-regulatory effects of GH; managed with metformin, GLP-1 analogues, or insulin alongside acromegaly treatment.
Colorectal adenomas and carcinoma — 2-fold increased colorectal cancer risk; colonoscopy surveillance every 5 years (or annually if adenomas found) recommended from diagnosis.
Sleep apnea — obstructive sleep apnea in 60-80%, often severe; increases cardiovascular risk and contributes to metabolic deterioration; requires polysomnography and CPAP treatment.

Hypopituitarism — compression or surgical damage to normal pituitary tissue causes deficiency of LH, FSH, TSH, and ACTH in up to 30% of patients; requires hormone replacement and regular dynamic testing.

Pituitary apoplexy — acute hemorrhage into the adenoma with sudden severe headache, visual loss, and adrenal crisis; occurs in approximately 2-12% of pituitary adenoma patients; constitutes a neurosurgical emergency.

supportResources.name: Acromegaly Community
supportResources.description: Patient community and educational resource for people living with acromegaly, with connection to patient advocates and physician directories.

supportResources.name: Pituitary Network Association
supportResources.description: Non-profit patient advocacy organization providing educational resources, physician referral support, and community connections for pituitary tumor patients including acromegaly.

supportResources.name: Endocrine Society — Acromegaly Patient Education
supportResources.description: Clinical-society patient page on acromegaly causes, diagnosis, and treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Non-functioning pituitary adenoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Non-functioning pituitary adenomas produce mass effects (headache, visual field loss, hypopituitarism) without hormonal excess syndromes. IGF-1 and GH are normal. Acromegaly requires biochemical excess — elevated IGF-1 and failure to suppress GH on OGTT — in addition to or independently of a pituitary tumor visible on MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism causes fatigue, weight gain, and periorbital edema that can be confused with early acromegaly features. Hypothyroidism is excluded by a normal TSH; IGF-1 is low or normal in hypothyroidism, not elevated as in acromegaly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cushing's syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cushing's syndrome (cortisol excess) produces central obesity, moon face, and buffalo hump — a very different morphology from the acral-facial overgrowth of acromegaly. Biochemically, Cushing's shows an elevated 24-hour urinary cortisol and failure to suppress on overnight dexamethasone test, whereas acromegaly shows elevated IGF-1 and non-suppressible GH.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary adenoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Acromegaly almost always arises from a somatotroph pituitary adenoma; managing the adenoma through surgery, medical therapy, or radiosurgery is the cornerstone of acromegaly treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Cushing's syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Another pituitary-origin endocrine excess syndrome (ACTH-producing adenoma causing cortisol excess) managed at the same pituitary multidisciplinary centers as acromegaly.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple endocrine neoplasia type I
relatedConditions.relevance: MEN1 syndrome includes pituitary somatotroph adenomas as one of its three cardinal features; all patients with MEN1 are screened for acromegaly with IGF-1 testing.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetes insipidus
relatedConditions.relevance: Transsphenoidal surgery for acromegaly can damage the posterior pituitary or pituitary stalk, causing transient or permanent diabetes insipidus requiring desmopressin treatment.

faqs.question: What is acromegaly?
faqs.answer: Acromegaly is a rare hormonal disorder caused by a benign pituitary tumor that produces excess growth hormone. This drives overproduction of IGF-1, causing progressive enlargement of the hands, feet, face, and internal organs. It affects about 40-70 people per million and is diagnosed on average 7-10 years after symptoms begin.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of acromegaly?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are gradual enlargement of the hands and feet, coarsening facial features with prominent jaw and brow, excessive sweating, headaches, joint pain, carpal tunnel syndrome, and sleep apnea. Many patients first notice rings or shoes no longer fitting before other features become apparent.

faqs.question: What causes acromegaly?
faqs.answer: In more than 95% of cases, acromegaly is caused by a benign growth hormone-producing adenoma of the pituitary gland. Rarely, it results from tumors elsewhere in the body that secrete GHRH, stimulating the pituitary. Genetic causes (MEN1, AIP gene mutations) account for 5-15% of cases.

faqs.question: How is acromegaly diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires an elevated IGF-1 level for age and sex, confirmed by failure of GH to suppress below 1 ng/mL during an oral glucose tolerance test. MRI of the pituitary then identifies the adenoma. Biochemical testing comes before imaging in the diagnostic sequence.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for acromegaly?
faqs.answer: Transsphenoidal pituitary surgery is first-line treatment, achieving remission in 75-90% of microadenomas. For residual or unresectable disease, somatostatin analogues (octreotide or lanreotide), the GH receptor blocker pegvisomant, or stereotactic radiosurgery are used.

faqs.question: Can acromegaly be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. Surgical removal of the pituitary adenoma cures acromegaly in 75-90% of microadenomas and 40-65% of non-invasive macroadenomas. Patients with larger or invasive tumors often require ongoing medical therapy with somatostatin analogues or pegvisomant to maintain normal IGF-1 levels.

faqs.question: Is acromegaly life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Untreated acromegaly shortens life expectancy by 10-15 years, mainly from heart disease, diabetes, and sleep apnea complications. With successful treatment bringing IGF-1 and GH to normal, life expectancy returns close to that of the general population.

faqs.question: What is the difference between acromegaly and gigantism?
faqs.answer: Both are caused by excess growth hormone from a pituitary adenoma. Gigantism begins before the growth plates close in childhood, causing extreme height increase. Acromegaly begins after growth plate closure in adults, causing enlargement of the hands, feet, and face without increase in height.

faqs.question: How do doctors test for acromegaly?
faqs.answer: The first test is a blood IGF-1 level. If elevated, a glucose tolerance test with GH measurements is performed: failure to suppress GH below 1 ng/mL confirms acromegaly. An MRI of the pituitary then identifies the tumor. Most patients need further tests to screen for complications including echocardiogram and glucose testing.

faqs.question: What complications does acromegaly cause?
faqs.answer: Key complications include cardiomyopathy with heart failure, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, sleep apnea, joint disease, colorectal polyps and cancer, and hypopituitarism. These drive the increased mortality in untreated acromegaly and require ongoing monitoring even after remission.

faqs.question: How long does it take for acromegaly symptoms to improve after treatment?
faqs.answer: GH falls within hours of successful surgery; IGF-1 normalizes within weeks to months. Soft tissue swelling in the hands and feet decreases over months to years. Carpal tunnel syndrome often resolves in 3-12 months. Facial features soften gradually but do not return to baseline.

faqs.question: What is octreotide and is it used for acromegaly?
faqs.answer: Octreotide is a somatostatin analogue given as a long-acting monthly injection. It suppresses GH secretion by blocking somatostatin receptors on the pituitary tumor, normalizing IGF-1 in 40-65% of patients. It is used after incomplete surgery and as primary therapy when surgery is not possible.

faqs.question: Do people with acromegaly have a higher cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Patients with acromegaly have an approximately 2-fold increased risk of colorectal adenomas and colorectal cancer. Colonoscopy surveillance from diagnosis — every 5 years if no polyps, annually if polyps found — is recommended by all major guidelines.

faqs.question: Can acromegaly be inherited?
faqs.answer: Most cases are sporadic with no family history. However, MEN1 syndrome and AIP gene mutations cause familial forms accounting for 5-15% of cases. Genetic testing is recommended for patients under 30, those with a family history of pituitary tumors, and those with multiple endocrine tumors.

faqs.question: Does acromegaly affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women may develop irregular or absent periods; men may develop erectile dysfunction or low libido. These effects result from hyperprolactinemia (co-secreted prolactin in 25% of adenomas) or hypopituitarism suppressing LH and FSH. Treatment of acromegaly often restores fertility in young patients.

metaDescription: Acromegaly: excess growth hormone from a pituitary tumor causes enlarged hands, feet, and jaw. Diagnosis, surgery, somatostatin treatment, and outlook reviewed.
